text: The surgeon insufflates carbon dioxide into the preperitoneal plane, visibly expanding the space under laparoscopic vision.
The white posterior rectus sheath is visible, with the cobweb-like spaces opening up in the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon identifies the white shiny posterior rectus sheath, ensuring to stay above it during dissection to avoid going too low.
The surgeon visually identifies the white shiny posterior rectus sheath, highlighting its presence in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and begins to develop the avascular plane in the preperitoneal space, preparing for further dissection.

The surgeon performs telescopic dissection to develop the pre-peritoneal space, visualizing the process under direct vision. A small branch from the inferior epigastric artery is visible during the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the white shiny posterior rectus sheath, ensuring to stay close to it while retaining vessels towards the anterior abdominal wall. The inferior epigastric vessel is left on the roof to facilitate easier dissection.
Under visual guidance, the surgeon identifies anatomical planes and continues the dissection in the pre-peritoneal space, maintaining proximity to the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon identifies the avascular space, ensuring that the rectus muscle is not exposed, with a small fascia left covering it.
The surgeon continues the dissection until the posterior rectus sheath is visible. Below the umbilicus, the fascia becomes thin, making the peritoneum susceptible to puncture.
The surgeon carefully identifies the rectus muscle, ensuring a small fascia remains intact, and navigates between the two layers of the transversalis fascia to avoid causing injury.
The surgeon visually inspects the rectus muscle, observing a small fascia covering it, and proceeds with further dissection in the avascular space.
The surgeon uses forward and backward movements with the telescope to enhance visualization of the surgical field, aiding in the identification of anatomical planes during dissection.
The surgeon has reached the pubic bone during the dissection, with the anatomical structure clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on dissecting and identifying the midline, ensuring careful separation of the anatomical layers to avoid injury.
The surgeon gradually dissects medially towards the opposite side, expanding the surgical field to create a wider space for dissection.
The surgeon moves towards the lateral portion of the dissection area, continuing to carefully manipulate the tissue.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection using gentle movements, carefully manipulating the tissue to avoid potential injury to the peritoneum.
The surgeon identifies a direct hernia, noting the absence of the cupous ligament in the view. Just lateral to this area, the surgeon enters an avascular plane.
The surgeon gradually expands the avascular plane adjacent to the direct hernia, using careful dissection to separate tissue layers.
The inferior epigastric vessels become visible as the surgeon continues the dissection in the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon stays close to the inferior epigastric vessels while gradually developing the dissection plane, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon uses force to open the fascia, which is fused close to the vessels, to enter the lateral space.
The surgeon carefully dissects the plane, revealing the edge of the peritoneum just below the inferior epigastric vessels. The dissection progresses higher to further identify anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon identifies the arcuate ligament and observes the fossa moving anteriorly and fusing. The vessel is pushed laterally to facilitate the insertion of a working port, aiming for optimal triangulation.
The surgeon introduces a small needle to verify that the peritoneum is not being punctured, ensuring direct access without injury.
The surgeon inserts the port at a calculated angle, ensuring optimal positioning for the procedure.
The surgeon inserts the port under direct vision, ensuring precise placement within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a low-profile hook instrument, likely for manipulating or dissecting tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses cautery to release and dissect thick fibrous strands or adhesions, carefully navigating in the avascular plane.
The surgeon navigates the avascular plane, which is visibly expanding due to the insufflation of carbon dioxide, allowing for clearer visualization of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies a vessel as part of the corona mortis, an anastomotic vein running along the cupous ligament between the external iliac vein and the obturator vein.
The surgeon continues the dissection using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques to carefully identify the correct tissue planes, minimizing bleeding to maintain a clear surgical field.
The surgeon observes the inferior aspect of the rectus muscle, noting its poor condition, indicating that the dissection has proceeded slightly anteriorly.
The surgeon observes the thin fascia in a very thin patient, highlighting the ease with which the surgical plane can be lost during dissection.
The surgeon uses a single-hand instrument to create space on the opposite side of the bilateral hernia under direct vision, preparing to introduce a working port for optimal triangulation.
The surgeon meticulously crosses the midline using short bursts of cautery combined with blunt dissection, carefully navigating the thin fascia in this patient.
The surgeon continues to open up the tissue planes, gradually progressing towards the opposite side of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses traction and carbon dioxide insufflation to reveal avascular planes, carefully navigating these areas to avoid bleeding or trauma to important structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the roof of the surgical field, ensuring the inferior epigastric vessels remain undisturbed and positioned correctly. Short bursts of cautery are used to manage fibrotic tissue as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery combined with blunt dissection to open up tissue planes, pushing the peritoneal flap down and expanding the surgical space into the lateral area.

The surgeon approaches the lateral border of the left rectus muscle, preparing for the appearance of the inferior epigastric vessels while ensuring dissection continues in the correct avascular planes.
The surgeon carefully retains a small fascia on the muscle while avoiding excessive dissection towards the muscle, preventing the inferior epigastric vessels from falling down.
The surgeon adjusts the plane of dissection to ensure the inferior epigastric vessels remain in the roof and are not inadvertently brought to the floor, maintaining their proper anatomical position.
The surgeon preserves a small fascia and continues the dissection just below it, ensuring the fascia remains intact while progressing through the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to ensure the inferior epigastric vessels remain positioned on the roof, maintaining their orientation during the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection near the lateral border of the left rectus muscle, ensuring the preservation of the fascia to prevent the inferior epigastric vessels from descending.
The surgeon identifies a small area of fibrosis within the surgical field, visible as a denser, fibrous tissue compared to surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a low monopolar cautery to perform sharp dissection, clearly revealing the avascular plane between two fascia layers. Fibrotic tissues are visible and are gradually dissected away.
The surgeon identifies a clear potential space between anatomical layers, preparing to enter and enlarge it to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon identifies fibrous bands in the midline that need to be dissected to ensure complete exposure of the bladder.
The surgeon dissects tissue away from the anterior abdominal wall, creating a space that allows for the placement of a mesh without rolling, ensuring proper positioning.
The surgeon observes increased fibrosis on the left side compared to the right, with visible fibrotic tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon takes down fibrotic attachments, clearing the space to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon identifies and prepares to remove the final fibrotic strands present in the surgical field.
The surgeon works adjacent and below the pubic symphysis, focusing on the area where fibrotic tissue has been dissected away from the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon removes the fibrotic strands and surrounding fat, revealing a clear view of the pubic symphysis and the arch of the bone.
The pubic symphysis becomes visible as the surgeon continues to remove fibrotic strands and fat, revealing the underlying bone structure.
The surgeon attempts to clear the area around the pubic symphysis by removing fibrotic strands, aiming for a clearer visual demonstration of the anatomical structure.
The surgeon continues dissection around the pubic symphysis, focusing on the cupus ligament to prepare the area for mesh fixation in the later stage of the procedure.
The surgeon employs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to open up tissue planes, revealing the area for surgical entry adjacent to the pubic symphysis.
The surgeon exposes the entire cupus ligament, a white shiny structure, making it clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgical field displays the 'white house sign,' characterized by a bright white light emanating from the area, indicating a specific anatomical landmark.
The video shows a comparison between the bone and the ligament, highlighting how the ligament reflects light brightly, unlike the bone.
The surgeon removes the remaining fibrous attachments, completing the dissection and exposing the area for entry.
The surgeon identifies the right side corpus ligament, which appears as a white shiny structure. The lighthouse sign is visible as the tissues are dissected and pushed away, reflecting bright light.
The shiny surface of the ligament reflects light back, creating a bright appearance.
The surgeon displays the fully dissected Cooper's ligament, highlighting its distinct appearance after separation from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon dissects the area below the pubic arch, ensuring at least three to four centimeters of clearance, to prepare for the placement of the mesh. The bone is exposed to allow the lower edge of the mesh to seat comfortably without rolling.
The surgeon meticulously dissects and removes the remaining fibrotic attachments in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection on the right side, revealing the absence of a direct hernia in the area.
The surgeon continues dissection to open up the lateral space, revealing the shiny, reflective surface of the dissected tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the inferior epigastric vessels, maintaining close proximity to these structures during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon opens up the potential lateral space using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques, revealing white tissue planes for further identification.
The surgeon uses monopolar energy at around twenty watts to coagulate small vessels, minimizing fogging in the preperitoneal area to facilitate continuous dissection.
The surgeon rapidly enlarges the lateral space, continuing dissection near the inferior epigastric vessels, with visible separation of tissue planes.
The surgeon carefully dissects around a small vessel, identifying the correct plane to avoid trauma to the surrounding peritoneum.
The surgeon uses a sharp C-monopolar hook to carefully dissect and remove fibrotic strands within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to coagulate small vessels while continuing dissection in the preperitoneal space, ensuring clear visibility and minimizing fogging.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneum, noting areas where the peritoneum is fused. Using a combination of energy devices, the team attempts to separate the fused peritoneum from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses short bursts of energy to release small tissue structures under direct vision, carefully separating them from surrounding areas.
The surgeon continues to dissect the peritoneum, working to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon attempts to identify the edge of the peritoneum on the left side of the patient, but it is not as clearly visible as it was on the right side.

The surgeon opens up the lateral space, preparing to insert the left working instrument. Both working instruments are inserted at or just below the level of the umbilicus, positioned on the lateral border of the rectus muscle.

The surgeon carefully dissects medially to the inferior epigastric vessels to avoid trauma and bleeding, ensuring the upper edge of the mesh is positioned correctly. The dissection progresses towards the camera port.
The surgeon dissects loose areolar tissue, continuing the process of separating the peritoneum from underlying structures.
The arcuate ligament is clearly visible on the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the maximum possible dissection in the surgical field, ensuring adequate space for further procedural steps.
A needle is introduced into the surgical field to identify the direction and orientation for further dissection or placement.

A 5mm working port is inserted on the left side, with its tip clearly visible. Using the assisting port, the surgeon performs blunt dissection in the lateral space of Bogros, applying traction and counter-traction to open up the tissue planes.
The surgeon applies good traction and counter traction to facilitate the dissection, allowing the tissue planes to open up smoothly.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic instrument in the left hand, applying pressure without opening the jaws to identify tissue planes. The instrument is also used to grasp tissues when necessary.

The edge of the peritoneum is visible, with the dulux fascia covering the nerves on the lateral side preserved. The muscles appear intact, and a fascia is seen on top. The surgeon identifies the edge of the mesh and attempts to retract it.

The surgeon dissects between two avascular planes at the edge of the peritoneum, using traction and counter traction to rapidly open the plane without causing bleeding. The attachments are being released, and the transversalis fascia is visible.
The surgeon releases fibrotic attachments to ensure the lateral edge of the mesh is seated comfortably without rolling, focusing on the area where the mesh attaches to form the arcuate line.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the inferior epigastric vessels, ensuring no trauma is caused to avoid bleeding. The pubic bone and arch are visible, along with the bladder in the surgical field.

The surgeon removes a few remaining fibers, revealing a clear view of the direct area with no hernia present. The lateral peritoneum, previously dissected, is visible alongside a large indirect hernia. The peritoneal edge and hernia sac are clearly observed.
The surgeon dissects and pushes the thin fibrovascular fascia surrounding the surgical area, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon has created a lateral space, allowing for a clearer view of the hernia sac, which is now more prominently visible.
The surgeon maintains the medial and lateral spaces open to facilitate further dissection, ensuring clear visibility and access to the surgical field.

The surgeon divides the transversalis fascia fibers, also known as the transversalis sling fibers, to expose the triangle of doom, allowing for clear dissection of the vas deferens and testicular vessels.
The surgeon identifies the pseudosac in the surgical field, highlighting its presence amidst the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon pushes the hernia sac upwards, attempting to completely reduce the hernia. Complete reduction is visually pursued to minimize the risk of seroma formation.
The surgeon employs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to manipulate the hernia sac, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic medline grasper to hold and manipulate tissues while progressing the dissection. Cautery is applied intermittently to aid in tissue separation.
The surgeon identifies the edge of the pseudosac, which is clearly visible and is being pushed upwards using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the hernia sac, using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques to carefully separate the sac from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins the sac dissection, approaching from lateral to medial, using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques.
The surgeon continues dissecting the hernia sac, progressing medially, which brings the vas deferens and testicular vessels into the field of view.
The surgeon identifies the white, shiny vas deferens as it becomes visible within the dissection field, progressing medially during the hernia procedure.
The surgeon has nearly completed the dissection of the hernia sac, with the sac fully exposed and ready to be hooked if necessary.
The surgeon considers dividing the peritoneum at the level of the internal ring, with the option to place an endo loop or ligature, leaving the distal sac intact.
The surgeon demonstrates the fully skeletonized hernia sac, grasping its edge and advancing the dissection further medially.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the white shiny vas, avoiding the use of electrical energy near the cord. Blunt dissection is primarily used, with occasional short bursts of energy applied when necessary.
The testicular vessels are now visible in the surgical field, appearing as distinct structures amidst the dissected tissue.
The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to carefully identify and push the testicular vessels and vas deferens away from the hernia sac.
The surgeon continues the dissection, gradually advancing further medially, with visible progress in separating the anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully identifies the hernia sac, ensuring it is fully dissected free and isolated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon ensures the hernia sac is empty of any contents before applying cautery, maintaining a clear view of the sac's interior.
The surgeon proceeds with sharp and blunt dissection techniques, avoiding the use of cautery due to suspicion, while carefully manipulating the tissue around the hernia sac.
The surgeon exposes the bluish peritoneum, indicating complete dissection around the hernia sac. The fascia is opened to ensure the sac is securely isolated and controlled.
The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to carefully separate fibrous tissue, minimizing trauma during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon isolates a large indirect hernia sac using short bursts of energy and blunt dissection, ensuring other structures are pushed away while dissecting around the sac.
The surgeon encounters a very thin fascia, making it easy to open the instrument and rapidly access the other side. The sac is fully encircled, allowing for easier retraction and expansion.
The surgeon identifies the fascia located between the hernia sac and the testicular vessel and vas deferens.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection around the hernia sac, carefully manipulating the tissue to avoid trauma.
The surgeon has successfully dissected around the hernia sac, revealing the opposite side of the surgical field, indicating complete circumferential isolation of the sac.
The surgeon has gained control of the vas deferens and testicular vessels, retracting them away from the hernia sac.
The surgeon follows the proximal sac distally, using short bursts of monopolar energy to dissect and push away small sling fibers, facilitating the identification and retraction of the sac's fundus.

The surgeon skeletonizes the hernia sac, carefully dissecting it to prevent bleeding while ensuring that vital structures such as the vas deferens and testicular vessels are kept away from the surgical field to avoid injury.
The surgeon identifies transversalis sling fibers within the surgical field, preparing to manipulate them as part of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon takes down and dissects the present sling fibers further away, facilitating a clear sac dissection and better identification of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the hernia sac, ensuring thorough separation from surrounding tissues rather than focusing on a single area.
The surgeon performs additional dissection to carefully push the testicular vessels away from the area being dissected, ensuring these vital structures are not damaged during the procedure.
The surgeon controls a small bleeding area using monopolar energy, ensuring hemostasis during the hernia procedure.

The surgeon identifies and controls a small vein, ensuring the vas deferens and testicular vessels are positioned away from the dissection area. The sac dissection continues, with adjustments made to the position on the thin peritoneum to identify the anatomical level of the structures.
The surgeon uses sharp bursts of cautery to further dissect the hernia sac, carefully changing positions to avoid injury to the thin peritoneum.

The surgeon identifies the fundus of the hernia sac, carefully dissecting away small fibrous attachments on top to prevent injury to the vas deferens and testicular vessels, ensuring a comprehensive dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection, gradually advancing further while ensuring the testicular vessels are kept away from the area of dissection.
The surgeon approaches the end of the fundus of the hernia sac, identifiable by its distinct bulgy U-shaped structure.
The surgeon performs a minimal dissection to reach the end of the hernia sac, preparing to fully extract it without needing additional dissection.

The surgeon carefully manages hemostasis while dissecting a large hernia sac extending into the scrotum. The fundus of the sac is visible, held in the surgeon's hands, with small fibrous attachments being addressed.

The surgeon releases the final fibrous attachments of the hernia sac, completing the dissection. The camera view is adjusted for clarity, revealing additional fibrous fasciae. The next step involves parietalization of the cord structures.

The surgeon carefully navigates the fibrous planes near the edge of the hernia sac, ensuring no undue tears occur. If a tear is present, suture ligation or an endo loop is used to secure hemostasis. The last few attachments of the sac are visible.
The surgeon uses a short burst of energy to coagulate remaining fascia near the car structures and vas, minimizing bleeding as the dissection continues.

The surgeon releases the last of the fibrous strands, completing the dissection of the hernia sac. Fumes are cleared by opening the gas through another working port, improving the visibility in the small surgical space.
The surgeon ensures good visualization as the last few fibrous attachments of the sac are carefully taken down, completing the dissection of the card structures.

The surgeon releases the last small fibrous strands between the fundus, freeing the entire sac. The sac descends into the abdominal area due to carbon dioxide insufflation, with no visible injury to the peritoneum.
The surgeon continues the dissection, focusing on the remaining fibrous attachments around the hernia sac, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
